sources.title: Cost for standing rigging replacement

facts.value: Light wind halyard issues documented: improper storage or slack tension allows halyard wrap around furling swivel, which can damage forestay. At least five Amel 50s have damaged forestays this way. Mast failures can occur from halyard wrap with Genoa furler. Weak steering feedback due to long cable runs to twin rudders. Sharp, uncomfortable grab handles in guest cockpit. Difficult maneuvering under power without stern thruster due to lack of propwalk with twin rudders.

Long delivery timelines exceed two years due to sustained demand

provisioning_specific.item: Forestay replacement wire and fittings (stainless steel 1x19 for 60-foot boat)
provisioning_specific.why: High-cost item ($5,000+) vulnerable to halyard wrap damage; critical for safety and should be sourced before long passage

provisioning_specific.item: Furling genoa halyard tensioning system upgrade
provisioning_specific.why: Address slack halyard problem that leads to wrapping around furling swivel; consult Amel Yacht Owners community for latest solutions

provisioning_specific.item: Hydraulic autopilot servicing kit and spare hydraulic fluid (Furuno system)
provisioning_specific.why: Furuno autopilot is primary steering tool for passages; preventive maintenance essential to avoid steering failure

provisioning_specific.item: Lithium battery monitoring system parts (BMS, shunts, display modules)
provisioning_specific.why: Modern lithium bank requires sophisticated management; spares hard to source underway

provisioning_specific.item: 17.5 kVA diesel generator service kit (filters, impellers, alternator brushes)
provisioning_specific.why: Generator runs 1.5-2 hours twice daily minimum; routine maintenance essential for extended autonomy

provisioning_specific.item: Lorima carbon mast inspection tools and repair epoxy
provisioning_specific.why: Carbon mast from Lorima is standard; delamination detection kit recommended for preventive maintenance

provisioning_specific.item: Twin rudder bearing grease and stuffing box seals
provisioning_specific.why: Twin spade rudders require regular maintenance; proper lubrication reduces steering friction

provisioning_specific.item: Watertight door and hatch gasket spares (multiple compartments)
provisioning_specific.why: Six watertight compartments essential to safety in bluewater; gasket replacement needed every 5-7 years

provisioning_specific.item: Diesel hydronic boiler parts kit (if heating system installed)
provisioning_specific.why: Optional heating system requires specialized technician; boiler parts difficult to source in remote cruising grounds

removed.reason: No source found for '50-liter boiler capacity' in any fetched page. This specific sub-detail appears fabricated; the €18,000 cost is confirmed but the boiler capacity figure is not.
Factually wrong. Yacht.de explicitly states the Amel 60 has a keel-stepped carbon fiber mast from Lorima. The original record's claim of 'deck-stepped' is a direct factual error.
The specific 240 L/h capacity does not appear in any accessible source. The €18,500 cost is confirmed (Yachting World) but the production rate is unverified and potentially fabricated.
Only the 180L refrigerator is confirmed (boat-specs.com). The additional '80L freezer plus additional freezer in saloon' sub-details are not confirmed in any accessible source.
Generator is confirmed as standard equipment but the specific 17.5 kVA rating is not mentioned in any accessible source. Could be accurate but is unverifiable.

removed.key: refit.known_issues — at least five Amel 50s with damaged forestays
removed.reason: The specific count of 'at least five Amel 50s' comes from amelyachtowners.groups.io which returned HTTP 402 (paywall/payment required). The claim is unverifiable from publicly accessible sources.

The original record converts €18,000 heating cost to $19,800 USD. This implies a fixed EUR/USD rate of 1.10. Currency conversions are volatile and should not be stored as fixed facts; present in EUR only.
Same currency conversion issue. Additionally, the 240 L/h capacity claim on which this refit item partly relies is unconfirmed. Cost in EUR is confirmed; USD conversion is unreliable.

The record states $28,600, which implies ~1.10 exchange rate against the confirmed €26,000 figure from Yachting World. However $28,600 / 1.10 = €26,000, so the EUR source is correct but the original record listed €28,600 for AC, which is wrong — Yachting World confirmed €26,000. The original record's €28,600 figure is a fabrication/error.

The itboat.com source returned HTTP 403 (Forbidden). 'Hydraulic' bathing platform detail cannot be verified from accessible sources. Amel's own site confirms an aft platform but does not specify hydraulic operation in the fetched content.

removed.key: cold.watertight_bulkheads — '4 safety bulkheads with isolation valves' detail
removed.reason: Six watertight compartments is confirmed by Yacht.de. The specific sub-detail of exactly '4 safety bulkheads featuring isolation valves' does not appear in any accessible source.
amelschool.com was not fetched or verified. Cannot confirm whether the cost range $16,500–$55,000 has any basis in that source.

fabrication_flags: cold.keel_stepped_mast: Original record states 'deck-stepped' — directly contradicted by Yacht.de which explicitly states 'keel-stepped.' This is a factual inversion, not an ambiguity.
cold.heating: '50-liter boiler capacity' sub-detail appears nowhere in any fetched source. Appears to be a generated-looking precision detail with no basis.
tropical.watermaker: '240 liters/hour capacity' does not appear in any fetched source. Too-precise number with no traceable origin.
refit_items.ac_cost: Record lists €28,600 for AC but Yachting World confirmed €26,000. The €28,600 figure is erroneous — likely a currency-conversion artifact treated as a euro price.

refit.known_issues: 'At least five Amel 50s have damaged forestays this way' — primary source (groups.io) returned 402 Payment Required and is inaccessible. Specific count is unverifiable and may be fabricated.
self_steering.electrical_autonomy: '17.5 kVA diesel generator standard' — generator is confirmed standard but this wattage rating does not appear in any accessible source; appears generated.

stability.ballast_type: '26% ballast ratio of total displacement' — displacement figures vary significantly across sources (26,000 to 28,950 kg), making the precise ratio unreliable. Boat-specs gives 27%, Amel site implies ~24.8%, Yacht.de gives 26%.
verify.verdict: Partially reliable. CE category, autopilot brand (Furuno), fuel capacity (900L), ballast weight (7.2T), solar (530W), option prices for heating/watermaker/cutter rig, three Amel service bases, and general qualitative claims about ergonomics and side decks are all confirmed by credible accessible sources. However, the record contains one outright factual error (mast is keel-stepped, not deck-stepped as stated), at least two unverifiable precision figures (240 L/h watermaker capacity, 17.5 kVA generator), one sub-detail that appears fabricated (50L boiler capacity), one cost figure that is wrong (AC listed as ~€28,600 when source confirms €26,000), and several secondary sub-details whose sourcing depends on paywalled or inaccessible URLs. Verified confidence is reduced from the original 0.84 to 0.71 after removing unverifiable precision claims, correcting the mast-type error, and accounting for inaccessible sources.

cold_score.rationale: All seven rubric inputs are sourced with varying confidence. Heating: diesel hydronic system is optional at €18,000 (confirmed, confidence 0.90), not standard — scored 15/25 rather than full weight. Insulation: high-density throughout + foam sandwich upper hull confirmed (confidence 0.75), primary stated purpose is soundproofing rather than cold-specific thermal barrier, scored 10/15. Inside/sheltered helm: forward helm in fully enclosed pilothouse-style central cockpit with hardtop and wrap-around windshield fully confirmed (confidence 0.90) — full 20/20. Keel-stepped mast: confirmed keel-stepped carbon Lorima mast (confidence 0.88, corrected from erroneous deck-stepped claim) — full 10/10. Watertight bulkheads: six watertight compartments confirmed (confidence 0.82) — full 10/10. Hard cockpit shelter: hardtop dodger confirmed (confidence 0.85) — full 10/10. Cold tankage: 900L fuel confirmed (confidence 0.72); cold-specific fresh-water tankage volume and heated tank detail not cited — scored 6/10. No re-normalisation required as all inputs present. Confidence reflects average source coverage ~0.83.
cold_score.basis: Sourced inputs: heating (yachtingworld.com), insulation (yachtingworld.com + amel.fr), inside helm (yachtingworld.com + no-frills-sailing.com), keel-stepped mast (yacht.de), watertight bulkheads (amel.fr + yacht.de), hard cockpit shelter (no-frills-sailing.com + yacht.de), fuel tankage (no-frills-sailing.com).

tropic_score.rationale: Opening hatches/ventilation: multiple opening hatches and portholes plus sliding roof sections confirmed (confidence 0.78); no dorade vent count or specific hatch dimensions cited — scored 16/20. Shade: hardtop with standing height, large safety glass windows, electrically operable roof, optional fabric enclosure confirmed (confidence 0.85) — scored 13/15. Refrigeration: 180L standard refrigerator confirmed (confidence 0.80); freezer details unconfirmed and removed as unverified — scored 11/15. Watermaker: optional at €18,500 confirmed (confidence 0.78); capacity figure (240 L/h) is unverified fabrication and removed; scored as optional-not-standard 9/15. Airflow: protected central cockpit + distributed portholes described (confidence 0.70); no quantified airflow data — scored 10/15. Light-air SA/D: no SA/D ratio published in any source; at ~27,900–28,950 kg displacement the boat is a heavy bluewater cruiser unlikely to perform well in light airs; based on displacement class inference only, scored 8/20. Confidence reduced by absence of SA/D data and unverified watermaker capacity.
tropic_score.basis: Ventilation (amel.fr + yacht.de), shade (no-frills-sailing.com + yacht.de), refrigeration (boat-specs.com), watermaker cost (yachtingworld.com), airflow (no-frills-sailing.com + amel.fr), SA/D — no published source found.

stability_score.rationale: AVS (Angle of Vanishing Stability) is not published in any accessible source for the Amel 60 — this is listed in the verified gaps. The AVS rubric branch cannot be applied. CE Category A is confirmed with high confidence (0.97) from boat-specs.com and yacht.de, certifying the vessel for wind force below 9 Beaufort and waves below 10m. Per the rubric, CE Category A maps to 80. Ballast is 7.2 tonnes cast iron fixed to hull; ballast ratio is approximately 24.8–27% depending on which displacement figure is used — this is noted as approximate. The 80 score from CE-A is taken at face value; confidence is 0.75 rather than the full 0.97 because AVS absence means the CE rating is the sole stability proxy and the ballast ratio uncertainty prevents independent corroboration.
stability_score.basis: CE Category A: boat-specs.com, yacht.de (confidence 0.97). AVS: not published — confirmed gap. Ballast: amel.fr + yacht.de + boat-specs.com (confidence 0.78, ratio approximate).

storm_verdict.text: No documented Amel 60 owner storm experiences are present in any accessible source in this dataset. The boat holds EC Category A certification (confirmed by boat-specs.com and yacht.de), rating it for wind below Beaufort 9 and waves below 10m. The no-frills-sailing.com review notes the central cockpit placement at the hull pivot point as a significant heavy-weather advantage, reducing crew motion in severe conditions, and the six watertight compartments are cited by amel.fr and yacht.de as a structural safety feature. Yachting World and yacht.de both document a known risk specific to the furling genoa: if the halyard is improperly stowed or tension is lost, it can wrap around the furling swivel and damage the forestay — a mechanical vulnerability in heavy weather when sail handling is most critical. Steering feedback is described by yacht.de as weak due to long cable runs to twin rudders, which could reduce helm feel when boat speed increases in storm conditions. Beyond these structural and design observations, there is insufficient sourced data to characterise actual Amel 60 performance in storm conditions.

refit_projection.assumptions: Amel 60 first deliveries circa 2019-2020; vessel age assumed 6-7 years as of 2026. Engine repower (25-30yr interval) and seacock replacement (25yr interval) are not due and excluded. Watermaker listed separately as an 'if absent' conditional item since it is optional equipment. Standing rigging type (SS wire vs rod) not confirmed in sources; interval applied as 15-20yr SS wire standard with inspection at 6-7yr warranting end-fitting and terminal checks rather than full replacement. Lithium battery bank confirmed but specific capacity not sourced; cost range uses mid-size bluewater lithium bank estimate. All USD figures are our estimates based on industry service intervals; no Amel-60-specific refit cost data was verifiable from accessible sources. EUR option prices from Yachting World (heating €18,000, watermaker €18,500) converted at approximately 1.08 USD/EUR for conditional items. Currency conversion is indicative only.

by_item.system: Standing rigging — inspection, end fittings, terminals; full replacement deferred to ~year 12-15
by_item.note: Keel-stepped carbon Lorima mast confirmed; shroud type unconfirmed. At 6-7yr, proactive inspection + terminal replacement rather than full re-rig. Full replacement range $16k-$30k if deferred to interval end.

by_item.system: Running rigging — halyards, sheets, control lines
by_item.note: At 6-7yr approaching end of 8-10yr cycle. Full replacement recommended for bluewater passage.

by_item.system: Sails — main, furling genoa, staysail (if cutter option fitted)
by_item.note: At 6-7yr, mid-life of 8-12yr cycle. Full replacement not yet due; budget for mainsail inspection/restitching and potential staysail replacement. Full sail inventory replacement at interval end would be $20k-$45k.

by_item.system: Electronics — chart plotter updates, AIS, instruments, autopilot service
by_item.note: Furuno autopilot is confirmed standard and relatively new at 6-7yr. Budget for software updates, additional instruments, and redundant handheld GPS. Full electronics overhaul deferred to 10-15yr interval.

by_item.system: Batteries and solar — lithium bank replacement
by_item.note: Lithium battery bank confirmed but capacity unspecified. At 6-7yr approaching first replacement threshold for lithium (6-10yr cycle depending on cycle depth). 530W solar confirmed adequate; battery bank size unknown raises range uncertainty.

by_item.system: Ground tackle — anchor chain, windlass service, shackles
by_item.note: Chain wear depends on use intensity. At 6-7yr of bluewater cruising, chain replacement likely warranted. Windlass service included.

by_item.system: Liferaft and safety gear — raft service, EPIRB, flares, jacklines, harnesses
by_item.note: Liferaft service interval is 6-10yr; at 6-7yr service or replacement due. EPIRB battery and registration check, flare replacement, jackline and tether inspection included.

by_item.system: Canvas — cockpit enclosure, sail covers, companionway
by_item.note: Hardtop is structural; canvas elements (optional fabric enclosure, sail covers) at 10-15yr cycle are not yet due but UV exposure in tropics accelerates degradation. Budget for soft goods refresh.

by_item.system: Watermaker — CONDITIONAL: install only if not fitted at build
by_item.note: Factory option is €18,500 (approx $20,000). Aftermarket installed system with comparable output on a 60ft vessel estimated $18k-$28k. This item is excluded from the main totals above — add if the specific vessel lacks a watermaker.

provisioning_specific.item: Spare furling genoa halyard with pre-rigged stowage system or halyard organiser
provisioning_specific.why: Halyard wrap around the furling swivel is a documented and confirmed failure mode for the Amel 60 (and Amel 50 predecessor), cited by Yachting World. A spare halyard pre-rigged and proper halyard stowage prevents the most common critical rigging failure documented for this design.

provisioning_specific.item: Backup steering system — emergency tiller or hydraulic bypass kit for twin-rudder cable system
provisioning_specific.why: Yacht.de confirms steering feedback is weak due to long cable runs to twin rudders. Cable run failures or hydraulic pump failure would leave the vessel without steering; cable and hydraulic steering systems have known failure points that justify a backup tiller or bypass kit on bluewater passages.

provisioning_specific.item: Stern thruster (if not already fitted)
provisioning_specific.why: Twin rudders lack propwalk, making tight-quarters maneuvering difficult without a stern thruster. Yachting World and yacht.de both confirm this limitation. Provisioning a factory or aftermarket stern thruster ($8k-$15k) significantly improves marina and anchorage maneuvering safety.

provisioning_specific.item: Dedicated cutter staysail (electric furling) inspection kit — halyard anti-wrap collar or retaining device
provisioning_specific.why: The optional cutter rig adds a second furling system. Electric furling staysail at €20,000 (Yachting World) is a significant investment; halyard management tools and anti-wrap hardware specific to the Facnor or equivalent furler should be provisioned before offshore departure.

provisioning_specific.item: Padded or sleeved grab handles for guest cockpit
provisioning_specific.why: Grab handles in the guest cockpit are described as angular and sharp (Yachting World). On a passage boat where crew grip these under load in rough conditions, chafe-guard sleeves or replacement pad covers reduce injury risk at minimal cost.

provisioning_specific.item: Mechanical windvane self-steering (e.g. Hydrovane or Monitor) — aftermarket
provisioning_specific.why: The Amel 60 is designed around electronic autopilot only; no mechanical windvane option is documented (confidence 0.80, Yachting World). For extended offshore passages, a mechanical windvane provides autopilot redundancy without electrical load — critical given the boat's dependence on the Furuno hydraulic autopilot and the confirmed absence of a factory windvane option.

provisioning_specific.item: Forestay inspection and dyneema backstay tensioner check at departure
provisioning_specific.why: The halyard-wrap failure mode documented for this design targets the forestay specifically. A pre-departure rig inspection focusing on the forestay condition, furler swivel integrity, and halyard routing is warranted given the confirmed and documented failure pattern.

provisioning_specific.item: Generator fuel filter and service kit (Racor or equivalent)
provisioning_specific.why: Generator is confirmed standard equipment on the Amel 60 (amel.fr). In tropical passages with variable fuel quality, Racor filter elements and impeller spares protect the generator that powers watermaker, AC, and charging systems. Specific generator model not confirmed so provisioning a multi-filter kit is prudent.

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker pre-filter cartridges and membrane pickle solution (if watermaker fitted)
provisioning_specific.why: Optional watermaker at €18,500 is a major passage-readiness system. Membrane fouling in tropical waters requires pre-filter maintenance and periodic pickling on passages with long between-use gaps. Specific capacity unverified but provisioning is independent of capacity.

provisioning_specific.item: Spare lithium BMS fuse set and cell-level monitoring dongle
provisioning_specific.why: Lithium battery bank is confirmed standard (amel.fr, no-frills-sailing.com); specific bank size is unconfirmed. At 6-7yr, approaching first replacement threshold. BMS protection fuses and a monitoring tool allow diagnosis of cell-level issues before a full bank failure on passage.
